name: Faunus
description: Faunus is an ancient Roman deity associated with the forest, plains, and fields. He is often depicted as a rustic god, protector of shepherds and farmers, and is closely linked to the natural world. Faunus is considered a prophetic figure, delivering oracles and guidance through dreams and rustling leaves. He is a deity of untamed wilderness, embodying the wild, instinctive forces of nature. Roman tradition also ascribes to him the role of a culture hero, credited with introducing agriculture and animal husbandry to the early inhabitants of Italy. He is the Roman equivalent of the Greek god Pan, sharing similar attributes and functions. His worship was widespread throughout the Italian peninsula, especially in rural areas, where he was venerated with festivals and sacrifices intended to ensure the fertility of the land and the well-being of livestock. The female counterpart of Faunus is Fauna, who shares similar attributes and is considered his wife or sister.
subtitle: God of the Forest and Fields

keyMyths.title: Faunus and Picus
keyMyths.description: One myth tells of Faunus pursuing the nymph Picus, who was transformed into a woodpecker by Circe after Picus rejected her advances. Faunus, distraught, used his prophetic abilities to discover the spell cast upon Picus. He then attempted to reverse the spell, but was ultimately unsuccessful, highlighting the limits of even divine power against the enchantments of powerful sorceresses. This story illustrates Faunus's connection to the natural world and the transformative power of magic.

keyMyths.title: Faunus' Prophecies
keyMyths.description: Faunus was known for delivering prophecies through dreams and oracles. People would sleep in his sacred groves, hoping to receive guidance from the god. These prophecies were often cryptic and required interpretation, reflecting the ambiguous nature of the wild and the difficulty in understanding the will of the gods. Faunus's prophecies were particularly sought by farmers and shepherds seeking advice on how to ensure the fertility of their land and the health of their flocks, reinforcing his role as a protector of rural life.

keyMyths.title: Faunus and the Lupercalia
keyMyths.description: Faunus is linked to the festival of Lupercalia, a Roman fertility rite celebrated in February. During this festival, priests of Faunus, known as Luperci, would run through the city striking people with goatskin thongs. This act was believed to purify and promote fertility. The association with Lupercalia underscores Faunus's role as a god of fertility and the wild, untamed aspects of nature that were essential for the prosperity of the Roman state. The festival highlights the connection between the divine and the everyday lives of the Roman people.

sources.author: Ovid
